key_insight: MoS2 in a biphenylene network exhibits extreme direction-dependent transport, with the armchair axis acting as a strong current path and the zigzag axis showing negative differential conductance.
engineering_relevance: This anisotropy enables orientation-specific nanoelectronic designs, including a reliable current switch along armchair and an NDC-based device along zigzag.
method: Electronic, thermal, thermoelectric, and I–V characterization
evidence_signal: At room temperature, phonon thermal conductance is very low (0.28 nW/K armchair, 0.23 nW/K zigzag), zT peaks at 0.27 and 0.19, and the current ratio reaches about 7×10^4 between armchair and zigzag directions.
watch_item: The results are theoretical and depend on idealized structure and orientation, so experimental synthesis, defects, and contact effects could change the observed anisotropy.
paper_title: Direction-Dependent Quantum Transport Properties of MoS$_2$ Integrated into Biphenylene Configuration
